coredata.dc:title: Prussian blue analogues of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanozymes with high peroxidase like activity for sensitive detection of glyphosate and copper
coredata.dc:creator: Ravikumar, A.
Kavitha, S.
Arul, Amutha
Rajaji, P.
G, Tamilselvan
Li, Xuesong
Wu, Beibei
Surya, Velappa Jayaraman
Tang, Jun
Sivalingam, Yuvaraj
Zhang, Zhen
coredata.dc:description: The rational development of efficient nanozymes for the colorimetric detection of targets is still challenging. Herein, Prussian blue analogues of Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes were fabricated for colorimetric detection of glyphosate and copper ions owing to its peroxidase like activity. At the sensing system, the Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes display high peroxidase activity, which could catalytically oxidize the colourless TMB to blue colour oxTMB. In presence of glyphosate in this sensing system the blue colour is diminished, ascribed to the inhibit the catalytic activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes. Concurrently, the addition of copper ion, which result in blue colour was reappear due to the generation of glyphosate-copper complex formation. The Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes based colorimetric sensing platform was developed to sensitive detection of glyphosate and copper ions with low detection limit of 3 nM for glyphosate and 3.8 nM for copper. This method also displays satisfactory outcomes from real samples analysis and its good accuracy. Therefore, this work provides a great potential for rapid detection of the targets from the environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni-Co-MoS2 nano boxes
Peroxidase like activity
Colorimetric sensor
GLY and copper

Peroxidase like activity of Ni–Co– MoS2 nanoboxes

Mechanism and catalytic activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes
Colorimetric detection of GLY and copper
Application of GLY and copper in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) (b) The schematic diagram of the preparation of Ni–Co PBA and Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes.
(a–c) SEM and TEM images of Ni–Co PBA, (d–f) SEM and TEM images of Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB, H2O2, MoS2, Ni–Co PBA and Ni–Co–MoS2 system, (b) Peroxidase-like activity comparison of MoS2, Ni–Co PBA and Ni–Co–MoS2 system, (c) (d) Feasibility analysis of Ni–Co–MoS2 system with addition of GLY and copper.

Steady-state kinetic analysis of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes. Kinetic catalytic of TMB (a) and H2O2 (c) concentration. (b, d) Lineweaver-Burk plots of the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equations for H2O2 and TMB.
The proposed detection mechanism for detection of GLY and copper using Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes.

(a) The colorimetric responses of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes to the addition of GLY with increasing concentrations, (b) The relationship between absorption intensity and GLY, (c) Interference experiments of GLY with OPPs and common ions. (d) The colorimetric responses of copper ions with increasing concentrations, (b) The relationship between absorption intensity and copper ions, (c) Interference experiments of copper with other metal ions.
Comparison of analytic performance of copper and GLY with other methods.
Recovery and Relative Standard Deviations (RSD, n = 3) for detection of Cu2+ in real samples.
Recovery and Relative Standard Deviations (RSD, n = 3) for detection of GLY in real samples.

head.ce:title: Prussian blue analogues of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanozymes with high peroxidase like activity for sensitive detection of glyphosate and copper

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational development of efficient nanozymes for the colorimetric detection of targets is still challenging. Herein, Prussian blue analogues of Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes were fabricated for colorimetric detection of glyphosate and copper ions owing to its peroxidase like activity. At the sensing system, the Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes display high peroxidase activity, which could catalytically oxidize the colourless TMB to blue colour oxTMB. In presence of glyphosate in this sensing system the blue colour is diminished, ascribed to the inhibit the catalytic activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes. Concurrently, the addition of copper ion, which result in blue colour was reappear due to the generation of glyphosate-copper complex formation. The Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes based colorimetric sensing platform was developed to sensitive detection of glyphosate and copper ions with low detection limit of 3 nM for glyphosate and 3.8 nM for copper. This method also displays satisfactory outcomes from real samples analysis and its good accuracy. Therefore, this work provides a great potential for rapid detection of the targets from the environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Highly sensitive and accurate detection of targets is crucial for conducting biological and chemical analysis of the environment [1], diagnosing human health diseases [2], and ensuring food safety [3]. Presently, it is imperative to employ robust detection techniques capable of identifying even minute quantities of lethal viruses and toxic substances. The ability to sensitively detect trace levels of heavy metals and pesticide residues carries substantial implications for environmental preservation [4].

Glyphosate (GLY) is a widely used organophosphorus pesticide in agriculture and non-agriculture fields, known for its effectiveness in controlling unwanted plant growth [5–8]. With its extensive application in crop production, the consumption of GLY has steadily risen over the years. However, the accumulation of GLY residues in the soil can lead to the complex formation of transition metal ions, presenting significant risks to human health and the environment [9–14]. To address these concerns, the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) have established limits for GLY concentrations in drinking water at 0.9 μg/mL and 0.7 μg/mL, respectively [15,16]. Thus, accurate detection and identification of GLY are crucial for effective environmental monitoring. Various analytical methods have been developed for GLY analysis, including gas chromatography [17], high-performance liquid chromatography [18], electrochemical sensing [19], and fluorescence sensing [20]. Among these approaches, the colorimetric sensing method utilizing nanozymes has gained considerable attention due to its simplicity, affordability, direct signal readout, and high sensitivity. This method offers as a rapid and promising tool for detecting GLY.

Recent advanced nanoscience and nanotechnology have a major role in various fields including biosensing, energy storage, catalysis, and artificial nanozyme [21–23]. Nanomaterials act as artificial nanozyme with inherent catalytic activity and overcome the natural enzyme properties. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) as a very interesting porous nanomaterials and used wide ranging applications in gas storage, catalysis, biomedicine, and biosensor [24,25]. Prussian blue analogue is one type of metal organic framework used metal ions and cyanides [26–30] and high catalytic activity. The catalytic activity of Prussian blue analogues is primarily based on their surface properties. Therefore, surface modification is effective strategy to enhance catalytic activity [31]. Accordingly, the preparation of prussian blue analogues (Ni–Co PBA) and incorporated to MoS2 nanosheets. The introduction of MoS2 nanosheets in Ni–Co PBA is effectively enhance the catalytic performance and this hybrid nanostructure holds great promise in achieving high catalytic activity.

In this study, nanoboxes structured Ni–Co–MoS2 was synthesized by solvothermal method. The resulting Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes exhibited significantly enhanced peroxidase-like activity and suitable for the detection of GLY and copper. It was found that GLY inhibited the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes. However, the addition of copper ions to the sensing system restored the catalytic activity due to the formation of a GLY-copper chelate complex. Based on the mechanism is used for the detection of GLY and copper with high sensitivity and selectivity and applied for real sample analysis with good recovery results. This colorimetric sensor demonstrates that great potential for environmental and food safety monitoring.

Potassium hexacyanocobalt (III), sodium citrate, thiourea, ammonium molybdateand potassium acetate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Beijing, China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethyl benzidine (TMB), glyphosate and copper nitrate were obtained from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China) Co., Ltd. Acetic acid (HAc) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.
Ni–Co PBA was synthesized according to the previous report with modification [23].

. In brief, firstly prepared a solution A and B, nickel nitrate (0.6 mmol) and sodium citrate (0.9 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL distilled water (Solution A). Potassium ferrocynaocobalate (III) (0.4 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water (Solution B). The solution A and B were mixed thoroughly and magnetic stirring for 18 h at room temperature. Ni–Co PBA was collected by centrifugation and washed with distilled water and ethanol to remove unreacted reactants, finally dried at 70 °C at overnight.

60 mg of Ni–Co PBA and 20 mg of ammonium thiomolybdate were dissolved with 60 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) [32]. After ultrasonic treatment for 15 min, then the above mixture was transferred to 100 mL autoclave at 210 °C for 20 h. After that, the final products were collected by centrifuge and washed with distilled water and ethanol for three times.

The crystal phase of the products was recorded by PAN analytical X'pert pro-Xray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. High resolution scanning electron microscope (HR- TEM, Thermo scientific Apreo S) and High-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL Japan, JEM-2100 Plus) were used to study the morphology of the samples. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Agilent Technologies) was used for the analysis of the functional groups of the synthesized samples. The absorbance spectra were obtained using a UV–Visible spectrometer (Agilent Technologies).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase mimetic activity of the synthesized nanozymes was studied by using TMB and H2O2 substrates. In a colorimetric enzyme assay, 200 μL of TMB and H2O2 (6 mM) were mixed with 3 mg of Ni–Co–MoS2 in 1.5 mL of acetate buffer. After that, the reaction solution mixture was incubated at different time intervals and absorbance was measured by wavelength at 652 nm. Furthermore, the optimization study of concentration of substrate (TMB and H2O2), weight of nanozymes and pH were investigated by above mixture solution at optimized time. The enzyme kinetic parameters of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanozyme were obtained at Lineweaver-Burk plot, which were calculated by the double reciprocal plotting, including Michaelis-Menten equation (Km) and reaction velocity (Vmax) Michaelis-Menten equation:
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max
where V is initial the velocity, V max is the maximum velocity of the reaction, S is the initial concentration of the substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant that indicates the affinity of the enzyme to the substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of GLY and copper
ce:section.ce:para: A colorimetric detection of GLY and copper was developed by based on the nanozyme of Ni–Co–MoS2. In a typical procedure, 2 mL of Ni–Co–MoS2 (2 mg/mL), 50 μL of H2O2 and 500 μL of TMB solution were mixed with acetate buffer solution. The mixed solution incubate for 3 min at room temperature and addition of different concentration of GLY was taken into 1.5 mL cuvette, the absorbance spectra were measured at 652 nm. With the sensor system of Ni–Co–MoS2/TMB/H2O2, the detection of copper as follows: 2 mL of Ni–Co–MoS2 (2 mg/mL), 50 μL of H2O2 and 500 μL of TMB, GLY (0.1 mM) solution and different concentrations of copper were mixed with acetate buffer solution is placed in 1.5 mL cuvette. The absorption intensity was measured at a wavelength of 652 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GLY and copper in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed sensing system was used to detect GLY and copper in different real samples, including environmental samples and food samples. The environmental samples (Tap water, river water and lake water) and food samples (cucumber and cabbage) were collected from near our university campus. The collected cucumber and cabbage were crushed and mixed well and diluted by using acetate buffer solution and centrifuged several times and collected the supernatant solution. After that collected samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane and diluted to 10 times. Various concentrations of copper (5, 10 and 15 μM) and GLY (1, 2 and 3 μM) were added to the real sample solutions and measured by using UV absorbance at 652 nm. The recovery rate and RSD was determined by comparing the measured copper and GLY concentration in the spiked concentration. All the above experiments were repeated three times. We also explore the high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) as a reference method to evaluate the real sample analysis and compared to proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of Ni–Co– MoS2 nanoboxes
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis approach for Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, Ni–Co PBA is synthesized through the co-precipitation method by reacting nickel ions with the linker potassium hexacyanocobaltate (III) and the capping agent sodium citrate, resulting in the formation of nanocube structures of Ni–Co PBA (Fig. 1a). The Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes are synthesized by combining Ni–Co PBA with ammonium thiomolybdate under solvothermal conditions (Fig. 1b). Fig. 2 presents high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Ni–Co PBA and Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes.

In Fig. 2a, the SEM image of Ni–Co PBA nano cubes reveals a smooth surface and uniform cubic size and TEM images of Ni–Co PBA nano cubes (Fig. 2b&c) is clearly depicting that cubic structure. Furthermore, Fig. 2d, e, and f shows that SEM and TEM images of Ni–Co–MoS2, clearly indicating that ultrathin sheet like structure of MoS2 is assembled on the surface of Ni–Co PBA. This can be compared to the SEM image of pristine MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. S1). The above characterization is conformed to the successful synthesis of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes , clearly demonstrating the cubic structure with MoS2 nanosheets, which can be further utilized for peroxidase mimetic activity.

The XRD pattern of the Ni–Co PBA nano cubes and Ni–Co–MoS2 nano boxes (Fig. S2a). The diffraction peaks of Ni–Co PBA can be indexed well to the Ni3 [Co (CN)6]2 phase, confirming a pure cubic structure without any additional peaks. The diffraction peaks of Ni–Co–MoS2 shows a shift towards lower diffraction angles compared to those of pristine MoS2 (Fig. S3) and most of the peaks correspond to MoS2 nanosheets. In Fig. S2b, the FTIR spectra of Ni–Co PBA and Ni–Co–MoS2 are displayed. The main peak observed in both spectra corresponds to the CN stretching vibration at 2183 cm−1, indicating the formation of the Ni–CN–Co complex. The metal cyanide peaks are observed at 562 and 453 cm−1. Additionally, the peaks at 1721 and 742 cm−1 indicate the Mo–O and Mo–S stretching vibrations, respectively. These findings provide compelling evidence for the successful incorporation of MoS2 and Ni–Co PBA in the Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted to investigate the chemical state of the elements in the Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes compounds. Fig. S4a presents the Ni 2p spectra, where the binding energy at 855.9 eV and 872.3 eV corresponds to Ni(I), while the binding energy at 856.3 eV corresponds to Ni(II). Additional peaks observed can be attributed to satellite peaks of the corresponding Ni species in Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes. The Co 2p spectra can be divided into two peaks: 797.1 eV and 781.6 eV, which are assigned to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2, respectively. Additionally, peaks with binding energies at 802.9 eV and 789.7 eV correspond to satellite peaks, confirming the presence of Co species (Fig. S4b). In Fig. S4c, the Mo 3d-S2s spectra show a peak at 226 eV, which corresponds to the S 2s species. The two main peaks at 228.7 eV and 232.3 eV are assigned to Mo(IV). The S 2p spectrum exhibits two peaks at 161 eV and 162 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. S4d). The results obtained from SEM, TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS analyses collectively confirm the presence of Ni–Co PBA and the formation of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase like activity of Ni–Co– MoS2 nanoboxes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the peroxidase-like activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes, the peroxidase substrate used in this sensing system was TMB. As shown in Fig. 3 a&b, the UV-absorption spectra of bare TMB and the TMB/H2O2 system exhibited no colour change and no absorption peak. However, when Ni–Co PBA and MoS2 were added to the TMB/H2O2 system, a noticeable change in blue colour and a slight increase in absorption intensity at 652 nm were observed. Remarkably, the addition of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes to the TMB/H2O2 solution resulted in a significant increase in absorption intensity compared to bare MoS2 and Ni–Co PBA. This observation indicates that Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes exhibit high peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 3C, the absorption intensity decreased and colour of the solution is disappeared when the addition of GLY and subsequently addition of copper ions, the absorption intensity increased and blue colour was restored (Fig. 3d). This result demonstrated that Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes exhibited the high peroxidase like activity and sensitive detection of GLY and copper ions.

The optimization of this sensing system was investigated by the amount of catalyst, concentration of H2O2, pH, and temperature. The peroxidase-like activity of varying the amount of catalyst, the optimized amount of catalyst is 10 μg (Fig. S5a). The concentration of H2O2 was optimized to be 0.35 mM (Fig. S5b). The optimal conditions for pH (Fig. S5c) and temperature (Fig. S5d) were found to be 4 and 30 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme kinetic test of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes was evaluated through a steady-state kinetic study, which involved investigating various concentrations of TMB and H2O2 (Fig. 4 ). The Michaelis-Menten plot and the double reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plot were used to calculate the maximum velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km). The Lineweaver-Burk plot for TMB and H2O2 yielded Vmax and Km values of 0.54 μM/min and 1.2 mM for TMB, and 10.2 μM/min and 48.7 mM for H2O2, respectively. The lower Km and Vmax values of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes indicate a more effective enzyme-substrate binding affinity. To further assess the peroxidase activity enhancement, the Vmax and Km values of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes were compared with those of the natural enzyme HRP and a nanozymes (Table S1). Based on the results, the prepared Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes exhibited superior binding affinity and enhance the peroxidase activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and catalytic activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing material, Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes having the metal active sites of Ni2+ and Co2+, which could provide the higher oxidation-reduction reactions, therefore enhance the catalytic activity. The reaction system contains only TMB and H2O2, it showed no colour change of TMB after addition of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes into the reaction system, the colourless TMB is converted into oxTMB with blue colour. The addition GLY in this sensing system, the blue colour was diminished due to the GLY is adsorbed the surface of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes and inhibit the catalytic activity. At the same time addition of copper ions in above sensing system blue colour is reappear due to the formation of a GLY-Cu complex. The strong coordination ability of carboxyl, amine, and phosphate groups with copper suggests that when copper is introduced into the sensing system, GLY can completely bind with copper. These results indicated that Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes might possess peroxidase like activity along with rapid method for detection of GLY and copper ions (Fig. 5 ).

The catalytic activity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes confirms the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). During the catalytic reaction, H2O2 is easily decomposed, leading to the production of •OH radicals. To examine the generation of ROS species, scavenging experiments were conducted using IPA, PBQ, EDTA, and thiourea. Fig. S6a demonstrates that Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes exhibit high catalytic activity in the absence of scavengers. The addition of IPA decreases the absorption intensity, indicating a low quantity of hydroxy radicals in the solution. Similarly, the absorption intensity decreases upon the addition of PBQ. The absorption intensity dramatic decreased by the addition of thiourea, these results is confirmed by inhibition of peroxidase-like activity and the dominant role of •OH in the catalytic reaction. To further analyze the •OH in the catalytic reaction, a fluorescence probe experiment was conducted using terephthalic acid (TA) (Fig. S6b). TA captures the released •OH in the catalytic reaction, forming 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, which exhibits fluorescence emission at 435 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes is high compared to bare TA and H2O2, indicating the production of •OH during the catalytic reaction. Based on the above results, the Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes exhibited superior peroxidase like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of GLY and copper
ce:section.ce:para: The introduction of copper ions into the TMB/Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes/H2O2 sensing system plays a crucial role. GLY contains functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl, which have a strong affinity for coordinating with copper ions and forming GLY-Cu complexes. By incorporating copper ions into the sensing system, we expect that the GLY molecules will bind completely to the copper ions and reappearance of the blue colour (Fig. 5). To evaluate the sensor performance, different concentrations of copper were added to the system, and the resulting changes in absorption intensity were analyzed (Fig. 6d). As the copper ions concentration increased, the absorption intensity gradually increased until reaching a higher concentration, after which it returned to its initial state. Furthermore, a good linear relationship was observed for the detection of copper ions, and the limit of detection was calculated using 3SD/s (Fig. 6e). The limit of detection for copper ions was approximately 3.8 nM, which compares favourably to previous reports on nanozymes (Table 1). To assess the selectivity and specificity of the sensing system for detecting copper ions, competing metal ions were added to the system, and the absorption intensity was evaluated (Fig. 6f). The absorption intensity of copper ions significantly increased, while the other competing metal ions exhibited negligible changes. These results indicate that this sensing system enables effective and selective detection of copper ions. Furthermore, the synthesized Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes nanozyme exhibited excellent detection performance after repeating the experiments five times (Fig. S7a). The stability of the nanozyme was also investigated by storing it for 30 days (Fig. S7b), and no significant changes were observed during this period. This demonstrates the good cycling and storage stability of the nanozyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GLY and copper in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the effectiveness of our sensing system in real samples, we applied the Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes/TMB-based sensor to detect GLY and copper in different sample. The samples were collected from near university campus and local market. Based on the experimental results, we assessed the performance of our Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes/TMB sensing platform for the detection of copper and GLY in different real samples (as shown in Tables 2 and 3
). The results of the recovery rates for both methods were compared with different real samples. Table 2 shows copper recovery values 92–112 % for the proposed method and 94–102 % for the ICP-MS method. Table 3 shows GLY recovery values 92–112 % for the proposed method and 96–110 % for the HPLC-MS method. These results clearly demonstrate that effectiveness of our developed Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes/TMB-based sensor platform for the accurate detection of GLY and copper in real samples.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes that exhibit superior peroxidase-like activity for the detection of GLY and copper. This sensor demonstrates highly sensitive colorimetric detection of GLY and copper, offering good selectivity and minimal interference. Furthermore, the developed sensor is simple, rapid, and cost effectiveness. To assess the practicality of this sensor, we conducted real sample analysis using water and vegetables, obtaining good recovery results. Overall, the Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes-based sensor exhibits great promise for the detection of GLY and copper, achieving a low detection limit. This breakthrough opens new avenues for designing sensors based on Ni–Co–MoS2 nanoboxes to detect other targets in environmental and biological applications.

A. Ravikumar: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. S. Kavitha: Validation, Conceptualization. Amutha Arul: Validation, Conceptualization. P. Rajaji: Investigation, Formal analysis. Tamilselvan G: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xuesong Li: Software. Beibei Wu: Visualization. Velappa Jayaraman Surya: Visualization. Jun Tang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Yuvaraj Sivalingam: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Zhen Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Professor Zhen thanks to supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 21876067, 22176075). The Science and Technology Planning Social Development Project of Zhenjiang City (No. SH2021005). The Special Scientific Research Project of School of Emergency Management, Jiangsu University (KY–C-02 and KY-D-06). The Project of Research on Educational Reform and Talent Development, School of Emergency Management, Jiangsu University, and Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Material of Water Treatment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125542.
